action.reasoning: Observation: Two consecutive iterations segfaulted (exit 139) after ~800-960s with empty stdout. The custom SchNet model uses PyG's radius_graph and MessagePassing C++ extensions (torch_cluster), which are the most common source of silent CUDA segfaults in PyG. Analysis: To eliminate the C++ extension risk, I will replace the sparse graph radius_graph approach with a pure-PyTorch dense/loop model. Since QM9 molecules are tiny (~18 atoms), fully-connected pairwise message passing inside a Python loop over batch graphs is fast enough and completely avoids torch_cluster. Decision: Rewrite model.py as PureSchNet using torch.cdist and pure PyTorch ops (no radius_graph, no MessagePassing). Update train.py/predict.py accordingly. First run a tiny forward pass + 1000-sample training to verify no segfault and basic functionality.
action.command:
python -c "
import torch
from model import PureSchNet
m = PureSchNet(hidden_channels=64, num_interactions=2)
z = torch.randint(1,10,(50,))
pos = torch.randn(50,3)
batch = torch.cat([torch.zeros(20,dtype=torch.long), torch.ones(30,dtype=torch.long)])
out = m(z,pos,batch)
print('Forward OK', out.shape)
" && python train.py --epochs 1 --max_samples 1000 --hidden 64 --interactions 2

command_output.stderr: bash: line 10:   315 Segmentation fault      (core dumped) python train.py --epochs 1 --max_samples 1000 --hidden 64 --interactions 2
